original_question_text:
Read the poem 'Grandmother's Hands' and answer the question.

[Full poem provided above]

What does the speaker compare grandmother's hands to in the poem?

Why Our Neighbourhood Parks Are Worth Fighting For Every week, another green space disappears beneath concrete and steel. Developers promise progress, but what they deliver is the destruction of our community's lungs. We must act now to protect our neighbourhood parks before they vanish forever. Parks are not luxuries—they are necessities. Research from the University of Melbourne shows that children who play in green spaces for at least one hour daily perform 20% better academically and demonstrate significantly lower stress levels. These aren't just statistics; they represent real students achieving their potential because they had somewhere to run, climb, and breathe fresh air. Yet developers continue to target our parks. They claim that new apartment buildings will solve housing shortages, but at what cost? When Marrickville Council sold Patterson Park to developers in 2019, local doctors reported a 35% increase in childhood obesity within two years. The park had been the community's free gymnasium, its outdoor classroom, its meeting place. Now it's a carpark. Some argue that we need housing more than parks. This argument presents a false choice. Cities like Singapore have proven that we can build upwards whilst preserving green spaces. Their Housing Development Board mandates that every neighbourhood must have parkland within 400 metres of residential buildings. Why can't Australian councils adopt similar policies?

Read the poem 'Grandmother's Hands' and answer the question.

[Full poem: Grandmother's Hands]

What activity does the grandmother do with dough and flour?

Why Every Student Deserves a Later School Start Time Every morning, millions of teenagers around the world drag themselves out of bed in the dark, fighting against their own biology to make it to school on time. This daily struggle is not laziness—it is science. Schools must start later to protect the health and success of their students. The evidence is overwhelming. Research from sleep scientists shows that teenage brains are wired differently from adult brains. During adolescence, our internal body clocks shift forward by up to two hours. This means that asking a thirteen-year-old to fall asleep at nine o'clock is like asking an adult to sleep at seven o'clock. It simply does not work. Yet schools continue to ring the bell at eight-thirty or even earlier, forcing students to wake up when their bodies are still producing sleep hormones. What are the consequences of this mismatch? Students arrive at school exhausted, unable to concentrate or learn effectively. Studies across the United Kingdom, United States and Australia reveal that sleep-deprived teenagers perform significantly worse in mathematics and reading. Their grades drop, their creativity suffers, and their memory weakens. Is this really the best we can offer our young people?

Why Our Cities Must Ban Single-Use Plastic Bags Now Walk down any beach in the world and you will see them. Plastic bags tangled in seaweed. Plastic bags floating in the shallows. Plastic bags buried in the sand. These flimsy carriers, used for mere minutes, remain in our environment for centuries. It is time for every city to ban single-use plastic bags completely. Some people argue that plastic bags are convenient and cheap. They claim that shoppers need them to carry groceries home. But this argument ignores a simple truth: we managed perfectly well before plastic bags existed. Our grandparents used cloth bags and baskets. They survived. We can too. The environmental damage caused by plastic bags is staggering. Each year, Australians use approximately 3.6 billion plastic bags. Most end up in landfill or, worse, in our oceans. Marine animals mistake these bags for food. Sea turtles think they are jellyfish and eat them, often dying as a result. Birds feed plastic fragments to their chicks. Is our convenience worth the death of innocent creatures?

Based on the passage about William and his investigation of lightning, what can be inferred about the town council members who opposed William's proposal?

Refer to this passage:

In 1752, when ten-year-old William watched Benjamin Franklin fly his famous kite during a thunderstorm, he made a promise to himself: one day, he would measure the very thing that terrified most people. This fictional account, inspired by real events in early electrical science, explores how curiosity can transform fear into understanding.

William's fascination began not with wonder, but with trauma. A lightning strike had destroyed his family's barn the previous summer, killing three horses and nearly claiming his younger sister's life. While others in colonial Philadelphia saw the incident as divine punishment or random misfortune, William saw a puzzle. Why had the lightning struck the barn's tallest point? Why had metal tools near the impact site melted? Why had his sister, standing near the doorway, survived when the horses in the centre had not?

These questions consumed him. He began collecting observations from neighbours who had witnessed lightning strikes, carefully noting patterns in his leather journal. He sketched the paths of lightning bolts, measured the distance between strikes and thunder, and interviewed ship captains about lightning at sea. His mother worried that his obsession was unhealthy, a way of avoiding grief rather than processing it. His father, however, quietly encouraged the investigation, recognising that his son was transforming helplessness into agency.

William's breakthrough came not from dramatic experimentation, but from patient observation. He noticed that lightning consistently struck the highest metal points in the harbour—ship masts fitted with iron weather vanes. Yet wooden masts of equal height remained untouched. This pattern suggested that lightning was not random divine intervention, but a natural phenomenon following predictable rules. If metal attracted lightning, then perhaps metal could also redirect it safely away from vulnerable structures.

At thirteen, William proposed an ambitious project to the town council: installing metal rods on the church steeple, the tallest structure in Philadelphia, connected by wire to metal plates buried in the ground. The council was divided. Some members saw merit in the young man's careful reasoning and systematic evidence. Others viewed the proposal as dangerous arrogance, an attempt to interfere with heaven's will. The debate revealed a deeper tension in colonial society—the conflict between emerging scientific thinking and traditional religious worldviews.

The church elders ultimately approved a small trial on the parish barn. When lightning struck the rod during the next storm and travelled harmlessly into the earth, leaving the building undamaged, William felt no triumph, only quiet satisfaction. He had not conquered lightning or proven himself cleverer than nature. Instead, he had learned to listen to what the natural world was already teaching, transforming observation into protection.

Years later, as lightning rods became common throughout the colonies, William reflected that his greatest discovery was not about electricity at all. He had learned that the opposite of fear is not courage, but curiosity. By studying what frightened him, by asking questions instead of accepting explanations, he had converted a source of trauma into a tool for preventing others' suffering. The boy who once trembled at thunderstorms had become the man who taught his community to understand them.

Based on the passage 'The Unexpected Power of Urban Trees', what can be inferred about the relationship between tree coverage and energy consumption in urban areas?

When city planners design modern urban spaces, they often view trees as decorative additions rather than essential infrastructure. However, recent research reveals that urban trees provide far more than aesthetic appeal—they are silent guardians of public health and wellbeing.

A single mature tree can absorb up to 150 kilograms of carbon dioxide annually while releasing enough oxygen to support two people for an entire year. Beyond this gas exchange, trees act as natural air filters, trapping dust, pollen and pollutants on their leaves and bark. In heavily trafficked areas, strategically placed trees can reduce harmful particulate matter by up to 60 per cent, significantly improving air quality for nearby residents.

The benefits extend beyond respiratory health. Studies conducted in Melbourne and Sydney have demonstrated that neighbourhoods with abundant tree cover experience temperatures up to 5 degrees Celsius cooler than treeless areas during summer heatwaves. This cooling effect, known as the urban heat island mitigation, reduces the need for air conditioning and prevents heat-related illnesses among vulnerable populations.

Perhaps most surprisingly, urban trees influence mental health. Research from hospitals in the United Kingdom found that patients with views of trees from their windows recovered faster from surgery and required less pain medication than those facing brick walls. Similarly, students in classrooms overlooking green spaces showed improved concentration and reduced stress levels.

Despite these compelling advantages, many cities continue to prioritise development over tree preservation. As urban populations grow, recognising trees as vital infrastructure rather than optional decoration becomes increasingly critical. The evidence is clear: investing in urban forests is investing in healthier, more liveable cities for everyone.

Based on the passage 'The Library That Refused to Burn', what can be inferred about the ordinary citizens who participated in hiding the books?

In the spring of 1942, as Nazi forces tightened their grip on occupied Warsaw, a small group of librarians made a decision that would defy both logic and survival instinct. Rather than flee or destroy the precious volumes in their care, they chose to hide them.

The Central Library of Warsaw housed more than half a million books, including rare manuscripts, first editions, and irreplaceable historical documents. When German officials announced plans to confiscate or destroy Polish cultural treasures, head librarian Helena Kowalska understood what was at stake. Books were not merely paper and ink; they were the memory of a nation. To lose them would be to lose part of Poland itself.

Helena and her colleagues devised an audacious plan. Each evening, after the official closing time, they smuggled books out through basement tunnels that connected to neighbouring buildings. Volunteers risked their lives carrying bundles of volumes through the darkened streets to safe houses across the city. Some books were hidden in false walls. Others were buried in gardens or concealed in attics. A network of ordinary citizens—teachers, shopkeepers, students—became guardians of these literary refugees.

The operation required extraordinary courage and meticulous organisation. Every book removed had to be recorded so it could eventually be recovered. The librarians created coded lists, memorising locations in case written records were discovered. They prioritised works of Polish literature, history, and science—books that represented the nation's intellectual heritage. When German soldiers arrived to inspect the library, Helena calmly showed them depleted shelves, claiming that previous bombing raids had destroyed much of the collection.

The deception succeeded for nearly two years. Thousands of books survived because of these efforts, though the cost was devastating. Several members of the network were arrested and executed when their activities were discovered. Helena herself spent six months in prison, refusing under interrogation to reveal the locations of hidden books or the names of her collaborators.

When Warsaw was finally liberated in 1945, the city lay in ruins. The library building had been reduced to rubble. Yet slowly, books began to emerge from their hiding places. Families returned volumes they had protected. Workers excavating destroyed buildings uncovered waterproof containers filled with manuscripts. The coded lists, which Helena had somehow preserved, guided recovery teams to secret locations throughout the city.

Today, the reconstructed National Library of Poland houses many of these rescued volumes. Some still bear the scars of their wartime journey—water stains, torn pages, faded covers. But they survived, as did the culture they represented. Helena Kowalska's story reminds us that resistance takes many forms. Sometimes the most powerful act of defiance is not violence but preservation—choosing to protect what makes us human even when humanity itself seems under siege. The librarians of Warsaw understood that a nation without its books is a nation without its memory, and a people without memory cannot endure.

Read this passage:

'In 1826, a French inventor named Joseph Nicéphore Niépce created the world's first photograph. He used a special camera and a metal plate coated with chemicals. The plate had to sit in the camera for eight hours to capture just one image of his courtyard. Early photographs were called 'daguerreotypes' after Louis Daguerre, who improved the process in 1839. His method only needed fifteen minutes of exposure time. Before photography existed, the only way to record what someone looked like was through painted portraits. Photography changed everything, allowing ordinary people to preserve memories of their loved ones forever.'

How long did Niépce's plate have to sit in the camera to capture one image?

Read this passage:

'The Great Wall of China was built over many centuries to protect the country from invaders. Different emperors added new sections, making it longer and stronger. Workers used local materials like stone, brick and packed earth. The wall stretches across mountains, deserts and grasslands for thousands of kilometres. Soldiers lived in watchtowers along the wall, where they could spot enemies approaching from far away. They lit fires to send warning signals to other towers. Today, millions of tourists visit the Great Wall each year. It remains one of the most impressive structures ever built by humans.'

What materials did workers use to build the Great Wall?

Read this passage:

'In 490 BCE, a Greek soldier named Pheidippides ran from the battlefield of Marathon to Athens to deliver urgent news. The Greek army had just defeated the invading Persian forces, and the city needed to know they were safe. Pheidippides ran approximately 40 kilometres without stopping. When he arrived in Athens, he announced the victory with the words "We have won!" and then collapsed from exhaustion. Today, marathon races are held around the world to honour his legendary run. Modern marathons measure 42.195 kilometres, slightly longer than Pheidippides' original journey.'

What words did Pheidippides say when he arrived in Athens?

Read this passage:

'Creating a violin by hand is a skill that takes many years to master. A luthier, or violin maker, must carefully select wood that has been dried for at least five years. The top of the violin is usually made from spruce, whilst the back and sides come from maple. Each piece of wood is carved and shaped using special tools. The luthier then applies up to thirty layers of varnish to protect the wood and improve the sound. The entire process can take three to six months to complete. A well-made violin can last for hundreds of years and actually sounds better as it ages.'

What type of wood is the top of the violin usually made from?

Read this passage:

'The Trans-Siberian Railway stretches across Russia for 9,289 kilometres, making it the longest railway line in the world. Construction began in 1891 and took 25 years to complete. Workers faced enormous challenges, including freezing temperatures, dense forests and vast swamps. The railway connects Moscow in the west to Vladivostok on the Pacific coast in the east. Today, the journey takes about seven days if you travel non-stop. Passengers pass through eight different time zones and can see incredible scenery, from the Ural Mountains to the shores of Lake Baikal, the world's deepest lake.'

How long does it take to travel the Trans-Siberian Railway non-stop?

Read this passage:

'The Mystery of Yawning

Yawning is something everyone does, yet scientists still aren't completely sure why. When you yawn, you open your mouth wide and take a deep breath. One popular theory suggests that yawning helps cool down the brain when it gets too warm. Another idea is that it increases oxygen flow to the body. What researchers do know for certain is that yawning is contagious. If you see someone yawn, you're likely to yawn too. Even reading about yawning can trigger one! This happens because humans naturally copy the actions of others around them, a behaviour called mimicry.'

What do researchers know for certain about yawning?

Read this passage:

'Before computers, animators created cartoons by drawing thousands of pictures by hand. Each drawing showed a tiny change in movement. When these drawings were photographed and played quickly, one after another, they appeared to move. This technique is called 'frame-by-frame animation'. A single minute of cartoon required about 1,440 separate drawings! Famous early cartoons like Mickey Mouse were made this way. The artists worked in large teams, with some drawing the main characters whilst others filled in backgrounds. Today, computers can create similar effects much faster, but many animators still prefer the unique look of hand-drawn cartoons.'

How many separate drawings were needed for a single minute of cartoon?

Read this passage:

'Lake Baikal in Siberia, Russia, is the deepest lake on Earth. It reaches down 1,642 metres at its deepest point, which is more than five times the height of the Eiffel Tower. The lake is also incredibly old—scientists believe it formed around 25 million years ago. Despite being surrounded by freezing Siberian winters, Lake Baikal never completely freezes because of its enormous depth. The lake holds about 20 per cent of the world's fresh surface water, making it a vital resource. Over 1,700 species of plants and animals live in and around Lake Baikal, and many of these species are found nowhere else on the planet.'

How much of the world's fresh surface water does Lake Baikal hold?

Read this passage:

'The Journey of Chocolate

Chocolate begins its journey as cacao beans, which grow in large, colourful pods on cacao trees in tropical regions near the equator. After harvesting, farmers split open the pods and remove the beans, which are then fermented for several days. This fermentation process is crucial, as it develops the complex flavours we associate with chocolate. Once fermented, the beans are dried in the sun before being shipped to factories around the world. At the factory, the beans are roasted at high temperatures, then cracked open to separate the outer shell from the inner nib. These nibs are ground into a thick paste called chocolate liquor, which forms the base of all chocolate products.'

What happens to the cacao beans immediately after they are fermented?

Read this passage:

'The Desert's Water Collectors

In the arid Atacama Desert of Chile, one of Earth's driest places, fog catchers provide a remarkable solution to water scarcity. These simple structures consist of large mesh nets suspended between poles, positioned to intercept the thick coastal fog that rolls inland. As tiny water droplets from the fog collide with the mesh, they merge into larger drops that trickle down into collection troughs below. A single fog catcher can harvest up to 200 litres of fresh water daily, supplying remote villages that receive almost no rainfall. This ancient technique, once used by indigenous communities, has been modernised and now helps thousands of people access clean drinking water in one of the world's most inhospitable environments.'

What are fog catchers made from?

Read this passage:

'Before 1840, sending a letter was expensive and complicated. The person receiving the letter had to pay for it, not the sender. This caused problems because people often refused to accept letters they couldn't afford. A British teacher named Rowland Hill had a clever idea: create small stamps that senders could buy and stick on their letters. On 6 May 1840, Britain released the world's first postage stamp, called the Penny Black. It featured Queen Victoria's profile and cost just one penny. This simple invention made posting letters affordable for ordinary people and changed communication forever. Today, stamp collecting remains a popular hobby around the world.'

Who had to pay for letters before 1840?

Read this passage:

'The Deepest Lake on Earth

Lake Baikal in Siberia, Russia, holds the distinction of being the world's deepest lake, plunging to an astonishing 1,642 metres at its lowest point. This ancient lake formed approximately 25 million years ago, making it also the oldest lake on the planet. Despite being surrounded by frozen landscapes for much of the year, Lake Baikal never freezes completely due to its immense depth and volume. The lake contains roughly 20 per cent of the world's unfrozen freshwater—more than all the North American Great Lakes combined. Scientists regard Lake Baikal as a living laboratory because it hosts over 1,700 species of plants and animals, two-thirds of which exist nowhere else on Earth.'

How many species of plants and animals live in Lake Baikal?

Read this passage:

'The Secret Language of Bees

When a honeybee discovers a rich source of nectar, it returns to the hive and performs an extraordinary dance to share the information with other bees. The waggle dance, as scientists call it, communicates both the direction and distance of the food source. If the bee waggles in a straight line whilst moving upwards, the flowers are in the direction of the sun. The duration of the waggle indicates how far the bees must travel—a longer dance means a more distant location. Through this remarkable behaviour, thousands of bees can coordinate their efforts without any spoken language, demonstrating one of nature's most sophisticated communication systems.'

What does the waggle dance communicate to other bees?

Read this passage:

'The Great Barrier Reef, stretching over 2,300 kilometres along Australia's northeastern coast, hosts an extraordinary diversity of marine life. Among its most vibrant residents are the parrotfish, which play a crucial role in maintaining the reef's health. These colourful fish possess powerful beak-like teeth that enable them to scrape algae from coral surfaces. As they feed, parrotfish inadvertently consume small pieces of coral, which pass through their digestive systems and emerge as fine white sand. A single parrotfish can produce up to 200 kilograms of sand annually, contributing significantly to the formation of tropical beaches throughout the region.'

How much sand can a single parrotfish produce in one year?

Read this passage:

'The Floating Gardens of Bangladesh

In low-lying regions of Bangladesh, where flooding is common for months each year, farmers have developed an ingenious agricultural method called dhap cultivation. They construct floating gardens by weaving together water hyacinth and bamboo to form large rafts, then layering them with aquatic plants and soil. These buoyant platforms rise and fall with the floodwaters, allowing vegetables like gourds, tomatoes and leafy greens to thrive even during monsoon season. The gardens require no irrigation, as moisture seeps up naturally from below. When harvest time arrives, farmers navigate between their floating plots by boat, collecting produce that would otherwise be impossible to grow during the floods.'

What materials do farmers weave together to form the rafts?
